title: St. Archippus, St. Philemon, St. Apphia & St. Onesimus the Apostle
narrative:
St. Archippus the Apostle, St. Philemon the Apostle, St. Apphia the Martyr, and St. Onesimus the Apostle

On the twenty-fifth of Yekatit, the holy Church celebrates a remarkable and intimate assembly: four figures who are woven together in a single short letter of the Apostle Paul — the letter to Philemon — and whose intertwined stories constitute one of the most concentrated dramas of grace, freedom, and transformed relationship in the entire New Testament. Saint Philemon was a Christian householder of Colossae, a man of means and standing in his community, the host of the house-church that gathered in his home, and a person of such evident goodness that Paul addresses him warmly as a beloved fellow worker and a source of refreshment to the saints. His letter is the occasion for one of the most delicate diplomatic manoeuvres in Paul's correspondence: asking Philemon to receive back his runaway slave not as a slave but as a beloved brother.

Saint Onesimus is that runaway slave: a man who had fled his master's household for reasons the letter does not specify, had somehow encountered Paul in prison, had been converted by him to the faith, and had become, Paul says, his very heart — so valuable to the apostle in his imprisonment that he would have wished to keep him. Paul sends him back to Philemon not in chains but with a letter that is itself a charter of transformed relationship: he is sending him back, he says, as more than a slave — as a dear brother, both in the flesh and in the Lord. The Ethiopian tradition meditates upon this letter as a microcosm of the Gospel's social programme: not the abolition of social structures by decree but their transformation from within by the power of a love that renders the categories of slave and free secondary to the reality of brotherhood in Christ.

Saint Apphia, described by Paul as the sister in the faith, is generally understood to be Philemon's wife and therefore Onesimus's mistress — the person who, perhaps more than Philemon himself, needed to receive the returned slave with genuine charity rather than formal compliance. The tradition honours Apphia as a martyr and commemorates her alongside her husband as an equal witness to the faith, and this pairing is itself a statement: that the faith lived in the household is not the faith of the householder alone but of the whole family, that the transformation of relationship which Onesimus's return demands is as much her work as Philemon's, and that her martyrdom demonstrates that her faith was not merely domestic ornament but the real thing.

Saint Archippus, the fellow soldier whom Paul greets and whom the letter to the Colossians exhorts to fulfil his ministry, completes this domestic assembly with the apostolic vocation that reaches beyond the household into the wider community. Where Philemon's faith is expressed in hospitality and generosity, Archippus's is expressed in ministry — in whatever form of public service he had been entrusted with in the Colossian church. He and Apphia and Philemon and Onesimus together constitute a picture of the Church as the early tradition imagined it: gathered in a house, cutting across the sharpest social division of the ancient world (that between the free and the enslaved), practising the theology it preached in the most intimate and demanding arena of daily life. All four were eventually martyred, their household faith confirmed in the end by the same seal as the apostles who had formed them. Amen.
